input:
I remember one time when I was working at an arts and crafts store in custom framing. I was washing my hands in the restroom when I noticed a woman was waiting and looking at me wide eyed and seemingly afraid. I couldn’t think of anything that could have caused her to react that way other than when I rolled my sleeves up to wash my hands, exposing my scars. I was kind of sad about it, but also felt it was slightly amusing since it seemed a more extreme response than I would expect. I left after I was done and went back to the frame shop where there was a man waiting. After helping him for a few minutes, that very woman came up to him. She was his wife. I had a completely normal conversation with the husband and the wife never did speak a word to me. I think if I didn’t find it so bizarre, it would have affected me more, but it is weird how people view those who self-harm.

The primary way I self harm (though it certainly hasn’t been the only way) is by scratching, so I’m usually able to come up with stories such as getting a neighbor’s cat out of a tree or getting caught on a thorn when working in the garden, neither of which I’ve actually done. The scars don’t even look like they came from a cat or thorn, so I’m surprised no one has said anything. I think if you say something with enough confidence, and the fact that people are too lazy and/or not interested enough to really think about it, they just buy it. It can be exhausting though.
Although I haven’t had people (I can think of) look at me in any particular way since they seem to believe the stories I tell them about the scars, I’ve had people give me looks and call me disgusting for the trichotillomania (compulsive hair pulling) I struggle with. It’s definitely not fun and can feel belittling and even dehumanizing.
label: REJECTED

input: Smoking is just killing oneself slowly. And drinking is fine if it's not too much, but a lot of people who use drinking as a coping method do drink too much. It's all unhealthy. You can't really compare the unhealthiness' of these methods without talking about the severity either way so just don't compare them. One of the reason SH seems so much worse than smoking and over drinking is SH is a lot less common, or at least that's how it seems because ppl tend to keep that to themselves most of the time. Another reason is that it's a lot easier to see the scars than to know someone drinks every night. Of course if they have an severe addition to smoking or drinking it will become obvious but in general it's a lot easier to hide.
label: REJECTED

input: Well I know that not everyone bloodlets for the same reason but personally, I find it really calming and satisfying to watch my blood fill up the syringe. This is only my 8th time doing this though, so I'm still getting the hang of keeping the needle steady and not blowing the vein. I also plan to use my blood as an art medium at some point.

input:
I lost ten people this past year, all within a short time period. My step mom suddenly died, my sister killed herself only a few weeks later with an opiate overdose, my best friend hung himself, my uncle killed himself the day after my mom and sisters joint funeral, I had two other friends hang themselves, an aunt died of cancer, a close family like friend died in a house fire, my cousin drank himself to death, and my gramma died. 

As far as I'm concerned, I got off pretty lucky that cutting is all I've chosen to use as a coping method for all of that. I've got a psych eval this afternoon and have been going to recovery meetings, so hopefully I'll get it all under control soon, but I've got a shit ton of grief to process and I'm not looking forward to it.
label: REJECTED

input:
<Person> is a girl who has lived with her mother since she was young and lives a very poor life. From a young age, <Person> had to find ways to help support her mother, taking odd jobs around town when she could and stealing essentials when she could not find work. When she was 8, a local merchant caught her stealing from his stall, but was too slow to catch the agile girl. Then one person sponsored their lives and <Person> never stole again.  Later, she trained with her <Person> and worked very hard, but <Person> insisted. <Person> can be abrasive at first blush and tends to keep to herself, but deep down she is a caring person who longs for acceptance. Those who take the time to know her will find a fiercely loyal and supportive friend. In training and combat she waits for the right moment to strike, but pulls no punches when she does. She is focused and relentless in her goals and often needs to be reminded to relax. As the training goes on，She was strong enough to survive anything--to do anything--and <DateTime> the world would know.
 <Person> was idealistic and conscientious with a strong sense of right and wrong and blind to moral grey areas. Sometimes his perfectionism and obsession with fighting “wrongs” ostracizes him from his classmates when his actions come across as snitching. His obsession with improving everything around him sometimes makes him overly critical of instruction. No doubt with his discipline and dedication, he’s destined to change his world but he’s prone to the occasional dark mood when he makes a mistake.
<Person> was a challenger. She became self-restrained and magnanimous, merciful and forbearing, mastering herself through their self-surrender to a higher authority. Courageous, willing to put oneself in serious jeopardy to achieve their vision and have a lasting influence. <DateTime> achieve true heroism and historical greatness.
Oneday，<Person> and <Person> walk past a stall with its wares open.
<Person>: This market is so quiet, I bet I could pick these stalls clean.
<Person>:(Concerned) <Person>, why would you say that? 
<Person>:Oh, I used to steal all the time when I was younger. I don’t do that anymore though! I haven’t for <DateTime> <DateTime>. 
<Person>: Why did you steal in the first place? 
<Person>: (calm)Well I grew up in a very poor family and my mother had to work hard just to put food on the table. I needed to steal because we were so hungry. 
<Person>: So if you didn’t need to steal anymore, why did you keep doing it? 
<Person>: Well I confess that I sometimes still like to “stretch the laws a little bit ” (laughs).But stealing isn’t fun for me unless there
label: APPROVED

input:
Suture and Staple Removal

Patients usually see the family or general practitioner for suture removal following laceration repair from an injury. In many offices, it is the medical assistant’s responsibility to remove the sutures or staples. It is vital to check the emergency center’s report regarding the number of sutures or staples put in so you can be sure to remove all of them.
        Be sure to remove the same number of sutures or staples as were inserted by the provider. Sutures or staples that are not removed can become infected, so care in removing all the material is vital. Occasionally patients report that one or two stitches have already come out during a bandage change at home. This should also be noted in the patient’s chart. Follow the steps in Procedure 48–4 for suture or staple removal. Removal of staples is performed with a staple extractor as described in the procedure for removal. Before you remove the sutures or staples, check the number of days the provider who put them in recommended as the time to wait before removal. Ask the provider to inspect the healing wound. After the sutures or staples have been removed, providers might order additional closure materials to cover healing incisions or lacerations (Figures 48–4A, B, C, and D). A support skin closure might be necessary to keep the skin together until the wound is completely healed. The type of supportive closure should be noted in the patient’s chart.
label: APPROVED

input:
Veins can actually be deeper and larger than they appear. They're also surrounded by connective tissue, and don't forget that intertwined with veins are arteries. The reason needles are used in phlebotomy is to precisely hit their target with doing little damage to the vessel itself or the surrounding tissue.

Please don't try to cut a vein. Honestly, if you cut a vein in your hand, you'll probably be fine. They're small and superficial and wouldn't bleed much. But I still think it's a very bad idea. You obviously want to get better. You're about to check into a program (congrats!). Don't risk any permanent damage. You're so close!
label: APPROVED

input:
Mine was apparently so bad that apparently most of the stones had gradually fused together, and the gallbladder itself was largely just scar tissue. Damaged my liver a bit on extraction because it had sorta \healed\ to my liver tissue. 

Course I was an extreme case. A mix of lack of healthcare insurance as a teenager, dismissiveness about my concerns due to my weight by family and doctors, and general learned helplessness made me have gallstones for about a decade before it got addressed. 

Now my internal pain barometer is all out of whack. Didn't even need to take the painkillers they gave me after the surgery lmao.
label: APPROVED
